strategy_update: Three-phase approach: (1) Lock hard constraints first: place E10+E20 together in period 5 (C1), place E5 in period 1 and E6 in period 4+ (C2), place E3 in period 2 and E4 in period 6 (C3). (2) Distribute conflicting clusters using maximum period separation: E3/E4/E5/E6/E8 (heavy overlap) assigned to periods 2,6,1,4,7 respectively; E18/E19/E20 assigned to periods 3,8,5. (3) Fill remaining periods strategically using period-filling to balance room utilization and minimize secondary conflicts.

strategy_update: New strategy: (1) Identify and separate high-conflict exam pairs (E1-E2, E3-E5, E4-E5, E3-E4, etc.) into different periods. (2) Enforce hard constraints explicitly: E10-E20 in same period, E5 before E6, E3 and E4 in different periods. (3) Place large exams (E18, E8, E3, E19, E17 needing 48-60 seats) in different periods to avoid resource contention. (4) Use room constraints: E18 restricted to R5, other large exams use R3 or R5; spread these across periods to avoid overloading. (5) Reserve periods 1-8 (zero/low penalty) for all exams since period 9-10 are unavailable for most.

strategy_update: Identify exam conflict groups: (1) foundational courses with high enrollment overlap (E1,E2,E3,E4,E5,E6) requiring maximum separation; (2) CS progression courses (E9,E10,E15,E16) and their connectors (E11); (3) standalone courses (E7,E8,E12,E13,E14,E17,E18,E19,E20). Spread conflict group 1 across periods 1-6, respecting precedence (E5 before E6). Place group 2 in remaining slots, avoiding conflicts with group 1. Assign large exams (40+ students) to large rooms (R3=80, R5=100); medium (25-39) to R1/R2/R3; small (15-24) to R1/R2/R4. Respect period availability (E5 not in 8, E6 not in 1, E13 not in 4, E16 not in 1, E18 only R5). Verify no student conflicts in final assignment.

strategy_update: Implement a two-phase greedy assignment: (1) Identify exam groups by student overlap intensity and spread them maximally: separate E3-E4 (18 shared students) into non-adjacent periods, similarly spread E1-E2, E3-E5, E4-E6 conflict pairs. (2) Assign large exams (>40 students) first to high-capacity rooms (R3/R5) across early periods, then fill remaining periods with smaller exams. (3) For each exam, iterate through available periods in order and place it in the first period where no enrolled student has another exam—this guarantees zero student conflicts. (4) Verify room capacity >= exam student count before finalizing.

strategy_update: Use graph coloring to separate all conflicting exams: (1) Identify high-degree conflict clusters: {E1,E2,E5} must use 3 different periods, {E10,E15,E16} must use 3 different periods, ensure E10 and E20 are always together. (2) Place constraint-linked exams first: E10 and E20 in period 1, ensure E5 (period 3) comes before E6 (period 4), place E3 and E4 in different periods (2 and 1). (3) For remaining exams, use round-robin assignment to periods 1-4 while verifying no conflicting pairs share a period. (4) Assign a single room per exam based on student count and availability.

strategy_update: Use conflict-aware graph coloring: (1) Identify high-degree conflict nodes (E3 has 5 conflicts, E4 has 4) and assign them to early periods separated from their neighbors. (2) Enforce hard constraints first: place E10 and E20 together in period 1, ensure E5 in earlier period than E6, and separate E3 and E4. (3) Use a greedy period assignment that respects exam-to-exam conflicts (exams sharing 15+ students must be in different periods). (4) Assign large exams (40+ students) to large-capacity rooms (R3, R5); for medium exams (30-49), prioritize R3/R5 first, then R1/R2 if available. (5) Verify no two exams in the same period share an exam room.

strategy_update: To generate a valid schedule: (1) First place constrained exams together—assign E10 and E20 to the same period early (constraint C1). (2) Build a student conflict matrix by identifying which exam pairs share students, then use greedy assignment respecting these conflicts. (3) Enforce temporal ordering—place E5 in an earlier period than E6 (constraint C2). (4) Ensure E3 and E4 occupy different periods (constraint C3). (5) For each exam, try periods in order of availability, checking that no assigned exam in that period shares students. (6) Verify room capacity sufficiency before finalizing.

Priority order: (1) Lock E10+E20 together per C1, (2) Place E5 in earlier period than E6 per C2, (3) Separate E3 and E4 per C3, (4) Spread conflict cores: E1→P1, E2→P2, E3→P1, E4→P2, E5→P3, avoiding same-period conflicts, (5) Balance large exams (E8, E17, E18, E19) across periods 1-7, (6) Use R3/R5 strategically to avoid overloading single rooms.
